topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a suffocating tapestry of silk and forced smiles. I leaned against a marble pillar, nursing a glass of champagne that tasted suspiciously like regret. Then I saw him. Across the room, laughing with the groom’s father, stood Alex. The air instantly thickened, turning cold in my chest. I straightened up, letting my gaze lock onto him, refusing to look away as if my attention were a physical force. When our eyes met, I offered a small, tight smile—the kind you give someone you’ve already moved past—and then deliberately turned my attention back to the swirling crowd. I made a show of adjusting my jacket, every movement precise, every breath controlled. I didn't approach; I just existed in my space, a self-contained fortress against the sudden, unwelcome intrusion of his presence.

[story 2]

“Well, look what the tide dragged in,” I muttered to myself, swirling the ice in my cocktail. My stomach clenched, a familiar, unwelcome tightening. Then, there he was, standing near the buffet table, looking annoyingly polished. He caught my eye, and instead of flinching or looking away, I held his gaze steady, letting a flicker of something sharp and unyielding cross my features. I took a slow sip of my drink, letting the silence stretch between us, heavy and charged. When he finally made his way over, offering a strained greeting, I met his eyes with a cool indifference that felt like armor. My voice, when I finally spoke, was level, almost too calm, cutting through the ambient noise. “Fancy seeing you here,” I said, the words carrying a weight that suggested this encounter was entirely beneath my notice.

[story 3]

The music was too loud, the joy of the occasion grating on my nerves. I was observing the scene from the periphery, a silent sentinel. And then I spotted him. He was talking animatedly to a group of people, radiating an easy confidence that used to feel like a direct challenge. A slow, deliberate pace pulled me toward him. I didn't rush. I let the distance between us grow, savoring the tension building in the space. When I finally reached him, I stopped just short of invading his personal bubble. I looked him directly in the eye, my expression utterly neutral, a carefully constructed mask of complete disinterest. “Still managing to look like you own the place,” I commented, my tone dry, laced with a subtle, almost mocking amusement. I didn't wait for a response; I simply turned and walked away, leaving him standing there, momentarily caught off guard by the sheer lack of reaction I’d offered.
